question: Baking out a vacuum system is the process of heating the chamber to elevated temperature while pumping, to drive off adsorbed water and accelerate outgassing of H$_2$ from bulk metal. The typical bakeout procedure for a stainless steel UHV chamber is:

options: Heat to 150–250°C for 24–72 hours while pumping; bake all components that will be in vacuum including gauges, valves, viewports, and feedthroughs; cool slowly (< 2°C/min near 120°C to avoid water recondensation); activate ion pump and NEG during final cooldown
Bake at 450°C overnight; cool as quickly as possible to fix the low outgassing state
Bake at 100°C for 1 hour — sufficient to remove all outgassing from stainless steel
Bakeout is only needed for the first-ever pump-down; subsequent pump-downs do not require baking
passive.explanation: Bakeout physics: at room temperature, water is strongly adsorbed (sticking coefficient $\sim 1$). Above $\sim 120°$C, water desorption rate greatly exceeds adsorption — baking drives it off. H$_2$ diffuses from the bulk much faster at elevated temperature (diffusivity $\propto e^{-E_a/k_BT}$ with $E_a \approx 0.6$ eV for H in Fe). After bakeout: residual gas is dominated by H$_2$ at pressures $< 10^{-10}$ mbar. Rule of thumb: one decade improvement in base pressure per 24 h at 200°C vs unbaked.

active.prompt: Order the correct steps for baking out a new UHV chamber:

steps.text: Assemble chamber with new copper gaskets; connect turbo pump and roughing pump; check all flanges are torqued correctly
Rough pump to $\sim 0.1$ mbar; start turbo pump; rough out to $10^{-6}$ mbar
Wrap chamber in heater tape or place in oven; ramp temperature to 150–250°C at $< 2°$C/min; hold for 24–72 hours; keep turbo running throughout
Ramp temperature back down slowly ($< 2°$C/min below 120°C); at $< 10^{-8}$ mbar and $< 50°$C, start ion pump; activate NEG at 400°C then cool; isolate turbo pump; verify base pressure with BA gauge

active.explanation: Critical points: (1) Never bake with a rotary pump oil connected — it backstreams. (2) Degas the ion gauge during bakeout (electron bombardment heating). (3) Seal the leak valve and blank viewports not in use. (4) NEG activation requires the getter to reach 350–450°C — can be done with a resistive heater or with the bakeout oven if the NEG is included. After cooldown, base pressure should be 10–100× lower than before bakeout.

question: Not all components in a UHV system tolerate the same bakeout temperature. Maximum bakeout temperatures for common UHV components:

options: Stainless steel CF chambers: 450°C; copper CF gaskets: 450°C; all-metal valves: 250–350°C (manufacturer-dependent); Pyrex/borosilicate viewports: 200°C; glass-to-metal seals (kovar): 200–250°C; Viton O-rings: 150°C (limits whole system if present); SMA/BNC feedthroughs: 150–200°C
All UHV components can be baked to 400°C without damage
Viewports must be excluded from bakeout because they are not UHV-compatible
Metal gaskets cannot withstand bakeout and must be replaced after each bakeout cycle
passive.explanation: The limiting component sets the whole system's maximum bakeout temperature. Viton O-rings (if present, e.g., on a roughing port or rough valve) limit the bake to 150°C. All-metal valves with PEEK seats: 200°C. Ion gauges: 250°C (controlled separately). Channels for laser light through viewport flanges: bake viewports separately at their limit. In a system with Rb dispensers: the dispensers themselves can tolerate 200°C but should not be activated during bake-in. IOT (indium oxide) coated viewports (for spectroscopy) should not exceed 200°C to avoid ITO damage.

question: During bakeout, an RGA (residual gas analyser) is a valuable diagnostic tool. The mass spectrum evolves characteristically as the bake progresses:

options: Early bakeout: water (18 amu), OH (17), O (16), H (1) dominant; mid-bakeout: H$_2$ (2) and CO (28) rise as water falls; end of bakeout and cooling: H$_2$ (2) dominates; unexpected peaks (44 = CO$_2$, 28 = CO/N$_2$, 32 = O$_2$) may indicate leaks or contaminated sources
During bakeout, the pressure immediately drops to the final base value
Water at 18 amu only appears after bakeout is complete
CO$_2$ (44 amu) is always the dominant gas during and after bakeout
passive.explanation: RGA interpretation table: mass 2 = H$_2$ (dominant post-bakeout); 4 = He (leak indicator); 12, 16, 28, 44 = CO/CO$_2$ fragments (contamination or pump oil); 14/28 = N$_2$ fragments (air leak); 18 = H$_2$O (pre-bakeout, surface water); 32 = O$_2$ (air leak when $\sim 1/4$ the N$_2$ peak); 40 = Ar (air leak — Ar is $\sim 1\%$ of air). A clean bakeout ends with only mass 2 visible; any other peaks warrant investigation. Methane (16 amu) can indicate organics or pump oil backstreaming.

question: Titanium sublimation pumping (TSP) is often used during and after bakeout to boost pumping speed for reactive gases. A TSP consists of:

options: A titanium wire or rod heated by passing a large current (50–60 A) until Ti sublimes and coats the cold chamber walls; the fresh Ti film chemisorbs H$_2$, N$_2$, CO, O$_2$ at room temperature; fired every few days or hours depending on gas load
A heated titanium block that evaporates Ti atoms which ionise and are collected on a biased plate
A Ti-coated surface that is cooled to liquid nitrogen temperature to cryopump reactive gases
TSP uses photoionisation of Ti atoms to trap gas molecules
passive.explanation: TSP parameters: firing current 40–60 A for 1–2 minutes produces a fresh Ti film 5–50 nm thick. Pumping speed for H$_2$: $\sim 8$ L/(s·cm$^2$) of coated surface — a 10 cm diameter TSP shroud gives $\sim 600$ L/s. Does NOT pump noble gases (He, Ar, Ne) or CH$_4$ effectively — only reactive gases. TSP is complementary to NEG (which pumps at the NEG surface, no sublimation needed) and ion pumps (which can pump noble gases via burial). After TSP firing, the base pressure typically drops by 5–10× within minutes, then slowly recovers as the fresh film saturates.

question: In-vacuum optical components (mirrors, waveplates, lenses, retroreflectors) must be UHV-compatible and bakeable. Special considerations for optical elements inside a UHV chamber:

options: Fused silica substrates (not borosilicate) and ion-beam sputtered (IBS) coatings are preferred — no binders or organic compounds in the coating; maximum bakeout $\sim 200°$C (IBS coatings) or 150°C (ion-assisted evaporation); mount with UHV-compatible screws (stainless or titanium)
Standard coated optics from any vendor are suitable for UHV if cleaned with acetone
Optical cements (e.g., NOA) are UHV-compatible if used in small quantities
Antireflection (AR) coatings must be removed before placing optics in UHV
passive.explanation: UHV optics checklist: (1) Substrate: fused silica preferred ($< 10^{-11}$ mbar·L/(s·cm$^2$) outgassing). BK7 glass is borderline (slightly higher outgassing). (2) Coating: IBS (sputtered, no binders) > electron beam (may contain organics in binder layer). (3) Mounting: contact area minimised to avoid bimetallic stress; Torr Seal epoxy (for vacuum side bonds) has outgassing $\sim 10^{-8}$ mbar·L/s — use sparingly or avoid. (4) Cleaning: ultrasonic bath in IPA/acetone, then drag-wipe with cleanroom lens paper; no touching with bare hands after cleaning. (5) Bakeout: 150–200°C limit for most coated optics.

question: After a successful bakeout, the system is 'vented' to atmosphere only when necessary (e.g., for a component change). Best practice for venting and re-pumping a UHV system:

options: Vent slowly with dry nitrogen or argon (not air — avoids water adsorption); work quickly with clean gloves; re-seal; rough pump then turbo; bake again at 150°C for 24 h (a 'mini-bake') to recover UHV without a full re-bake
Vent with laboratory air — any gas works since the pump will remove it
After venting, a full 72-hour bakeout is always required to recover base pressure
Dry nitrogen venting is no better than air venting since both contain similar outgassing gases
passive.explanation: Why nitrogen vent: at room temperature, water adsorption on steel is nearly instantaneous (sticking coefficient $\approx 1$ for sub-monolayer coverage). Air at 50% RH contains $\sim 10^{22}$ H$_2$O molecules/m$^3$; in 10 minutes of exposure, $\sim 10^{15}$ molecules/cm$^2$ adsorb — a complete monolayer on every surface. Dry N$_2$ (< 1 ppm H$_2$O) reduces adsorbed water by orders of magnitude. Rule of thumb: 1 minute air exposure requires $\ sim 2$ hours extra bakeout; dry N$_2$ exposure requires 2–4× less. After a mini-bake, base pressures of $10^{-10}$ mbar are typically recovered within 24 h.
